// File: docs/issues/vunderstory-bootstrap-harness/S006-capability-map-view.md
---
id: S006
title: "Capability Map View"
type: AFK
status: done
parent: "https://github.com/usorama/vunderstory/issues/1"
blocked_by: [S002, S005]
tracks: [O1, O3, OUT1, OUT2, C1, C2, C3, FR2, FR3, NFR3, NFR4, AC2]
---

## What to build

Show the founder a capability map that connects objective, outcome, capability,
acceptance signal, and current work state. This is the visible anchor for why
work exists.

## Traceability

- Objectives: O1, O3
- Outcomes: OUT1, OUT2
- Capabilities: C1, C2, C3
- User stories: US4, US6, US7
- Functional requirements: FR2, FR3
- Non-functional requirements: NFR3, NFR4

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] Capability map is generated from PRD traceability.
- [x] Founder-facing copy explains capabilities in outcome language.
- [x] A capability can show linked work items and acceptance criteria.

## Verification

- [x] Structural check: map data links PRD IDs without orphan records.
- [x] Automated test: PRD fixture produces expected capability graph.
- [x] Runtime/manual proof: one capability is visible with outcome and acceptance signal.
- [x] PR-zero evidence required: screenshot or serialized map.

## Evidence

- Branch/worktree: `codex/s006-capability-map-view` at `.worktrees/s006-capability-map-view`.
- Structural contract: `docs/architecture/capability-map-contract.md` documents sources and map shape.
- Automated proof:
  - `npm run test:capability-map`
  - `npm run test:prd-index`
  - `npm run test:work-graph`
- Runtime/browser proof:
  - `node src/capability-map-cli.mjs --capability C1 --html > /tmp/vunderstory-s006-capability-map.html`
  - `playwright screenshot --viewport-size=1280,800 file:///tmp/vunderstory-s006-capability-map.html /tmp/vunderstory-s006-capability-map.png`
  - Screenshot showed `C1 Understand My Intent`, an outcome signal, objective `O1`, outcome `OUT1`, requirements, acceptance criteria including `AC2`, and linked work including `S004`.

// File: docs/architecture/capability-map-contract.md
# Capability Map Contract

Date: 2026-04-28
Issue: S006
Status: Bootstrap contract

## Purpose

The capability map is the founder-readable answer to "why does this work
exist?" It is generated from PRD traceability, not maintained as a disconnected
diagram.

## Sources

- PRD IDs and titles: `docs/prds/2026-04-28-vunderstory-bootstrap-harness.md`
- Objective/outcome/capability rows: PRD traceability table
- Linked work items: `docs/issues/vunderstory-bootstrap-harness/*.md`
- Work state: work graph adapter founder states

## Map Shape

Each capability contains:

- `id`
- `title`
- `founder_summary`
- `objectives[]`
- `outcomes[]`
- `requirements[]`
- `nfrs[]`
- `acceptance[]`
- `verification[]`
- `work_items[]`
- `acceptance_signal`

The map also records `links[]` and `orphans[]`. S006 requires no orphan PRD
links before the view is considered valid.

## Verification

Run:

```bash
npm run test:capability-map
node src/capability-map-cli.mjs --capability C1 --html > /tmp/vunderstory-s006-capability-map.html
playwright screenshot --viewport-size=1280,800 file:///tmp/vunderstory-s006-capability-map.html /tmp/vunderstory-s006-capability-map.png
```

The test proves:

- PRD traceability produces a capability graph;
- capability `C1` links to objective `O1`, outcome `OUT1`, requirements, `AC2`,
  and work item `S004`;
- no generated PRD links are orphaned;
- the HTML view renders founder-facing outcome language and linked work.

// File: docs/architecture/architecture.md (part 2)
  external-write, security, sensitive-memory, deployment, money, or
  project-truth decision that has not already been contracted.
- `handoff`: route to another role through the typed mailbox.
- `complete`: stop because the graph has no active or ready work.

Required inputs:

- Work graph state: active work, ready queue, blocked items, dependencies.
- Claim/worktree state: owner, branch, path, heartbeat, stale/cleanup status.
- Mailbox state: blockers, review requests, handoffs, approval needs.
- Evidence state: sensors, evaluator status, audit references, missing proof.
- Budget state: model route, time, spend, retry, concurrency limits.
- Policy state: approved envelope, safe auto eligibility, critical flags,
  rollback status.
- Runtime adapter event: Pi/OpenClaw lifecycle event, Codex Stop-hook event, or
  runner/scheduler event.

Required stops:

- No ready or active work remains.
- A required founder decision is pending because the action is outside the
  approved envelope.
- A dependency or prototype gate is unsatisfied.
- A run budget, retry limit, or concurrency limit is exhausted.
- A claim is stale, dirty, missing its branch, or missing its heartbeat.
- Evidence or sensor gates fail without a safe retry path.
- Required Settings/provider/runtime configuration is missing.

This contract lets Vunderstory keep work moving while keeping the founder-facing
reason legible: "continuing because S003 is ready", "blocked because S011 has no
proved provider route", "approval needed because this would push externally",
or "complete because the graph is empty."

## Context Contract

Context packs must be bounded and purposeful. They include:

- The exact objective, outcome, and capability being changed.
- The relevant PRD slice and acceptance criteria.
- The current work item and dependency state.
- Prior evidence and failed attempts.
- Scoped Hermes memory references with provenance.
- Optional bootstrapped user/persona context from approved local sources such as
  `auto-os`, with source and promotion status.
- Current code facts discovered for this task.
- Tool profile, model route, and run budget.
- Stop conditions and required evidence.

They should exclude:

- Unbounded raw logs.
- Superseded plans unless they explain a current constraint.
- Unscoped personal memory.
- Model reasoning that cannot be tied to evidence.
- Tool access unrelated to the current role.

## Tool Profile Contract

Tool count and tool choice are risk controls. Vunderstory should prefer
role-specific tool profiles over giving every agent every tool.

Vunderstory borrows Overstory's role shape and maps it onto Vunderstory-native
Pi/Codex execution profiles. The goal is to reuse the proven responsibility
split, not to make Overstory the required runtime or control plane.

| Role | Default Tool Shape | Disallowed By Default |
|---|---|---|
| Planner | read, search, graph, PRD, evidence | write code, deploy, external mutation |
| Builder | read, search, edit, test, local shell, evidence | deploy, secrets, destructive commands |
| Evaluator | read evidence, run app, browser/test tools | authoring the same code under review |
| Releaser | merge, release checks, rollback, audit | unverified code changes |
| Learning Analyst | trace read, classify, propose candidates | direct production mutation |
| Security Reviewer | read, scan, threat model, policy | silent secret changes |

Critical or destructive tools require explicit approval unless a future policy
proves a narrower safe auto path.

Overstory pattern mapping:

| Borrowed Pattern | Vunderstory/Pi Mapping | Adoption Level |
|---|---|---|
| Read-only coordinator | Planner role owns graph routing, context packs, and next action selection, with no code-write tools | Full responsibility split |
| Scoped builders | Pi/Codex builder runs claim one work item, one branch, one worktree, one evidence contract | Full responsibility split |
| Independent reviewers | Evaluator role judges output against acceptance criteria and evidence without authoring the same code | Full responsibility split |
| Typed coordination channel | Graph, claim, run, evidence, blocker, and handoff records are structured Vunderstory primitives | Full pattern, Vunderstory-owned storage |
| Merge queue and health | Release service gates merge/release/block decisions and exposes trace, cost, verification, rollback, and health | Partial in bootstrap, full as release matures |
| Instruction overlays | Tool profiles, context packs, model routes, and role prompts become explicit run inputs | Partial until Pi/Codex spike proves exact runtime hooks |

The merge/health pattern also includes stale worktree detection and branch
preservation: expired claims, missing run heartbeats, missing branches, or dirty
worktrees become visible blocked/cleanup-needed states instead of silent local
mess. Cleanup removes local worktree folders only after merge or abandon, while
branches, evidence, and audit history remain inspectable unless an explicit
retention policy says otherwise.

S030 must prove Pi/Codex guard enforcement, mid-run message delivery, transcript
parsing, and failure handling before any Overstory runtime surface can be
wrapped. If Overstory proves compatible, Vunderstory may wrap limited surfaces.
If not, Vunderstory still keeps the same role model and implements the
orchestration directly over Pi/Codex, worktrees, and the graph service.

## Typed Coordination Mailbox Contract

Vunderstory borrows Overstory's coordination-mail pattern, but the mailbox is a
Vunderstory-owned primitive rather than uncontrolled agent chat. The purpose is
to let just-in-time Pi/Codex agents coordinate through small, durable,
auditable messages tied to the graph, evidence, and policy state.

Bootstrap message types:

- `claim_requested`
- `claim_granted`
- `claim_released`
- `context_needed`
- `blocker_raised`
- `handoff_posted`
- `evidence_ready`
- `review_requested`
- `review_failed`
- `review_passed`
- `policy_decision_ready`
- `human_approval_required`
- `merge_ready`
- `release_blocked`

Every mailbox message records sender role, recipient role or queue, linked work
item, run, payload schema version, evidence references, status, and timestamp.
Agents may only read messages for their role, claimed work item, or assigned
queue. Founder-facing UI should summarize mailbox state as plain-language
status, blockers, handoffs, review requests, and approval needs.

The mailbox is not a swarm goal. It is a coordination layer for bounded work:
one small work item, one role, one context pack, one evidence contract at a
time.

## Policy And Approval Contract

Vunderstory uses three founder-facing action classes:

| Class | Meaning | Default Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Safe Auto | Less-than-critical, reversible, well-evidenced action | May proceed only when category-enabled and gates pass |
| Bulk Review | Similar low or medium risk actions with shared evidence | Founder can approve as a group |
| Critical Approval | High-impact, irreversible, external, sensitive, deployment, security, money, or project-truth change | Must stop for explicit approval |

Safe auto is eligible by policy but default-off per action category until
explicitly enabled. Safe auto requires:

- Sufficient historical observations or a deterministic local check. Bootstrap
  threshold: either a deterministic local check or at least three same-class
  observations across at least two sessions or days.
- Clear linked capability and expected outcome.
- Classifier confidence at least `0.85`.
- Passing sensors.
- Rollback path.
- Audit event.
- No critical policy flags.

If friction recurs after a safe-auto improvement, the candidate is downgraded
and re-enters the graph for review.

The full bootstrap threshold contract lives in
`docs/architecture/decisions/ADR-0003-safe-auto-policy-thresholds.md`.

## Atomicity, Idempotency, And Retry Contract

Agentic systems fail in partial states. Vunderstory must make retries safe.

- Every external write needs an idempotency key or equivalent duplicate guard.
- Every long run needs a checkpoint and resumable status.
- Every applied change needs a rollback or compensating action.
- Every retry must know whether the prior attempt wrote, partially wrote, or
  failed before mutation.
- Every stage reports count-in and count-out health.
- "Stage ok" is invalid when expected outputs are zero.

This directly addresses the learning-loop failure where observations existed
but specs, filed work, and queued improvements stayed at zero.

The known failure must be represented by the S032 immutable fixture before this
retry/count contract is considered complete.

## Cost And Exonomics Contract

Vunderstory should treat AI output as cheap to generate but expensive to trust.

Every run records:

- Model/provider.
- Reasoning effort or equivalent.
- Token and spend estimate where available.
- Tool calls.
- Runtime duration.
- Retry count.
- Verification cost.
- Human approval burden.
- Maintenance risk.

The useful metric is not "lines of code produced." It is verified outcome per
unit of cost, risk, and review effort.

## Learning Contract

Learning is a governed loop:

1. Observe conversations, runs, tool output, failures, approvals, corrections,
   and runtime signals.
2. Classify the friction by capability, objective, source, risk, and confidence.
3. Propose an improvement candidate with evidence, expected outcome,
   verification, and rollback.
4. Apply policy: safe auto, bulk review, or critical approval.
5. Execute through the same worktree and sensor loop as product work.
6. Measure whether the triggering friction class declines over 30 days.
7. If not, create a blocking work item.

Captured friction is not success. Only converted, verified, and measured
improvement counts.

Founder-facing learning history is not a static changelog. It is a recurrence
analytics surface:

- Show one compact trend per friction class, using small multiples when many
  classes exist so the founder can compare patterns without a crowded chart.
- Provide an aggregate "is the system getting better?" summary above the class
  trends.
- Let hover or click open the truth card for a point: observation, source,
  linked improvement, evidence, verification, policy decision, rollback, and
  30-day recurrence movement.
- Separate "measured but not fixed" from "fixed and recurrence declining."
- Never show an improvement as learned merely because a file changed; it must
  have evidence of use and recurrence movement or a pending measurement label.

## Second-Order Failure Coverage

| Failure Class | Architecture Response |
|---|---|
| Single-player interface trap | Project cockpit, PRD, capability map, approvals, future team-ready graph |
| Alignment window collapse | PRD and work graph before execution, visible gates, policy stops |
| Tacit knowledge barrier | Hermes context plus provenance, never memory-as-proof |
| Review theater | Evidence cards, sensors, independent evaluator, founder-readable risk |
| Local plan isolation | No hidden execution plans; work items and run contracts are visible |
| Multi-agent race conditions | Worktree-per-run, claims, ready queue, merge/release gate |
| Agent swarm drift | No free-form swarm chat; typed mailbox messages tied to work items, roles, evidence, and policy |
| Tool count ceiling | Role-specific tool profiles and least-sufficient access |
| Atomicity/idempotency failures | idempotency keys, checkpoints, rollback, count-in/count-out health |
| Context rot | bounded context packs and evidence summaries instead of raw logs |
| Goal drift | objective/outcome/capability anchors and stop conditions |
| Marginal cost mirage | run budget, verification cost, maintenance risk, outcome metrics |
| Vibe-coded slop | maintainability gates, evaluator review, capability acceptance |
| Token cost explosion | run budgets, model routing, concurrency limits |
| Shadow operations | tool registry, audit trail, critical external-write approval |
| Toil shift | sensors and evidence automate verification, UI absorbs complexity |
| Cognitive load sprawl | cockpit, plain-language state, hidden internal jargon |
| IP and compliance uncertainty | evidence and policy records, no compliance claim without expert review |
| Autonomous liability | critical approval gates and audit trail for high-impact decisions |
| Compliance lag | policy/version registry and standards watch as a future service |

## Product Onboarding Contract

A new Vunderstory-built product starts with:

1. Pain statement.
2. Audience and stakeholder map.
3. Objectives and outcomes.
4. Capability map.
5. Data and privacy classification.
6. Security and abuse-case sketch.
7. Compliance profile if relevant.
8. Repos, environments, and deployment targets.
9. Model and tool policy.
10. Role and coordination-mailbox policy.
11. Release and rollback posture.
12. Learning and recurrence metrics.

The product is not ready for autonomous implementation until these exist at a
thin but real level.

## Bootstrap Implications

The bootstrap harness must implement a minimal vertical slice of every core
service, not a polished version of one isolated component. The first proof is:

- Vunderstory can ingest the known learning-loop failure.
- It can classify the friction.
- It can generate a capability-linked improvement candidate.
- It can policy-classify the candidate.
- It can run or prepare the fix through a real worktree path.
- It can verify the result.
- It can record evidence.
- It can measure recurrence.
- Its local issue board stays synchronized with `/to-issues` local markdown
  slices and status changes.

Before code-producing implementation starts, the bootstrap must also have the
manual worktree protocol, persistence ownership decision, safe-auto threshold
contract, and early learning-loop fixture represented in the issue graph.

## Non-Goals For Bootstrap

- Full hosted team platform.
- Legal compliance certification.
- Replacement of Hermes or Pi.
- Direct production mutation for critical actions.
- Fake dashboards over mocked core loops.
- Claude-only architecture.
- Unbounded multi-agent swarms before worktree, policy, and merge semantics are
  proven.

## Open Architecture Spikes

- Pi SDK runtime/provider smoke tests.
- Hermes install, memory scope, gateway, scheduler, and self-evolution check.
- Beads graph adapter and founder-facing state mapping.
- Overstory pattern/mail assessment with Pi/Codex runtime behavior.
- Learning-loop immutable fixture and replay path from observed broken run.
- Tool profile and tool-count ceiling tests.
- Idempotency and retry design for external writes.
- Context-pack compaction and memory conflict rules.
- SSDLC standards mapping for products that require stronger assurance.

## Source Grounding

- Vunderstory PRD: `docs/prds/2026-04-28-vunderstory-bootstrap-harness.md`
- Runtime eval PRD:
  `docs/prds/2026-04-29-runtime-eval-control-plane-and-autoresearch.md`
- Runtime eval architecture:
  `docs/architecture/runtime-eval-control-plane-and-autoresearch-contract.md`
- Architecture map: `docs/architecture/vunderstory-system-map.html`
- Research: `docs/research/`
- NIST SSDF SP 800-218: https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/218/final
- OWASP SAMM: https://owasp.org/www-project-samm/
- OWASP ASVS: https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
- NIST AI RMF: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework
